document.critique: Rechecked capacities, all pairwise restrictions, implications, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 3 satisfies every rule with three jobs per machine; its costs sum to 138. The other listed feasible-looking alternatives are either invalid or costlier, so this exact assignment and cost should be retained as the optimum.

Tested all listed assignments against capacities, eligibility, interactions, implications, and both exactly-one rules. Candidate 1 survives every constraint and totals 155. Candidate 2 violates the J1=X implication; candidate 3 violates J9 eligibility; candidate 5 violates the W exactly-one rule; candidate 0 exceeds X capacity. Candidate 4 also survives at 155, so the stated uniqueness is false; this valid minimum-cost proposal is retained despite the tie.

Tested machine counts, all interaction rules, both exact-one constraints, and recalculated costs. Candidate 6 survives every test with three jobs per machine and total 168. Candidates 3–5 fail conditional or capacity constraints; surviving candidates 0, 1, 2, and 7 cost 182, 176, 179, and 172. Therefore candidate 6 replaces the preceding proposal.

I rechecked capacities, all interactions, both exact-one rules, and recalculated costs. Candidate 6 is feasible with three jobs per machine and costs 137. Candidate 0 is feasible but its stated cost is wrong (actual 141); candidates 1, 4, 5, and 7 violate constraints or capacities, while candidates 2 and 3 cost more. Thus candidate 6 survives and is the unique minimum.

Rechecked capacities, all interactions, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 4’s assignment survives, but its listed cost is wrong: 17+3+18+16+32+10+3+5+5+18+1+24 = 152. The other candidates are either infeasible or costlier, so this corrected total replaces the preceding proposal.

Tested machine counts, all pairwise/conditional rules, and both exact-one constraints. Candidate 0 fails because J4=W and J7=W; candidate 5 fails the W exact-one rule; candidate 6 violates three-jobs-per-machine. Candidates 1, 2, 3, and 4 survive, with costs 153, 155, 169, and 158. Thus Candidate 1 is the unique lowest-cost feasible assignment.

Re-evaluated all decisive statements directly. Candidate 6 is consistent after correcting F to truthful: F’s statement evaluates true. The listed candidate’s F=liar therefore fails. The other near candidates fail decisively at A, D, I, O, or Q. The returned assignment satisfies each speaker’s statement/type equivalence and replaces the preceding proposal.

I evaluated every candidate against all 17 nested statements. Candidates 0–4, 6, and 7 each fail at least one direct truth-value equivalence. Candidate 5 survives: each statement evaluates true exactly for its truthful speaker and false for its liar speaker, including the decisive J, L, and Q checks. Therefore this exact assignment should replace the absent preceding proposal.

I re-evaluated every nested statement under the strongest candidate. Candidate 0 satisfies all statements except M: M's XOR evaluates true, so M must be truthful, not a liar. Changing M does not disturb any other statement (including K), and the corrected assignment satisfies all 17 equations. Thus this exact answer replaces the preceding proposal.

Re-evaluated every candidate against all 17 self-referential statements. Candidates 1–7 each fail at least one direct statement; Candidate 0 satisfies every equation except its listed P type. Under Candidate 0’s values, P’s IFF evaluates true, so P must be truthful. Flipping P does not alter any decisive downstream evaluations, yielding the unique consistent assignment returned here.

Re-evaluated all candidate assignments by substituting each type into its speaker’s full Boolean statement. Candidates 0–3 fail at least one speaker equation; candidates 5–7 also fail directly. Candidate 4 survives every equation, including F’s crucial AND structure and Q’s nested IFF/XOR, so it is the unique consistent answer.

I re-evaluated the decisive A and B formulas under inclusive OR and standard XOR/IFF. Candidate 0 fails immediately on A, while several alternatives fail on A or B as well. No preceding proposal was supplied; retaining the strongest candidate available, Candidate 0, is therefore provisional rather than fully verified.

I rechecked the modulus constraint: 101 appears as a reported remainder, so M must be 103. Testing the recurrence equations across repeated n mod 4 classes yields a near-consistent branch with t=2, r=2, s=1, but the supplied terms produce a one-unit mismatch at n=9, indicating an apparent data inconsistency. Among candidates, [49,50,59] is the surviving intended continuation; the others do not fit this branch.

Exhaustive checking over M ∈ {97,101,103}, all allowed periodic p,q,t values, and r,s ranges identifies a single system satisfying every supplied term. Extending that system gives 29, 74, 38. The other candidates fail either the fitted recurrence or the uniqueness constraints, so Candidate 0 is retained.

Re-derived the decisive constraints from n=4,8,12,16: they force M=97, t=-2, p[0]=2, q[0]=-1, r=2, s=3. Checking the other residue classes remains consistent; applying the resulting recurrence gives n=18,19,20 = 60,51,52. Thus Candidate 0 survives exact verification; the others fail immediately at n=18 or are invalid remainder forms.

Re-derived the system by comparing the n≡0 (mod 4) equations across four periods. Only M=101, p0=-1, q0=2, t=1 satisfies both independent congruences; it then gives r=3 and s=-2. Solving the remaining residue classes gives (p1,q1)=(1,-2), (p2,q2)=(-1,1), and (p3,q3)=(-2,2), all verified against available terms. These produce 13, 10, 55, so Candidate 1 is retained.

I rechecked the repeated-residue recurrence constraints across n=4, 8, 12, and 16 for all three possible moduli and coefficient ranges. The preceding review offered no proposal; candidates [0,0,0] and [-1,-1,-1] do not fit the observed modular transitions. The surviving verified continuation is [6,73,84], so Candidate 0 should be retained.

I rechecked the recurrence across all four residue classes and tested the candidate systems against the observed terms. Candidates 0, 2, and 3 fail immediately as unsupported repeats or invalid remainders. Candidate 1 does not satisfy the same modular coefficient constraints consistently. Candidate 4 survives the recurrence verification and yields the next terms [34,70,20], so it replaces the preceding proposal.
